title: Incursa.Quic Public API Boundary Architecture
purpose: Describe how the public listener, connection, stream, option, and error surface stays thin while the existing internal protocol runtime remains hidden behind a consumer-facing facade.
design_summary: The consumer API is a thin facade over the existing internal protocol engine. Public listener, connection, stream, option, and exception types own lifetime and configuration capture, while the packet parser, TLS bridge, handshake coordinator, key schedule, transport helpers, and runtime reducers remain internal. Async lifecycle entry points forward cancellation and terminal-state outcomes through the facade instead of exposing scheduler or transport state. The server callback surface is limited to selecting server connection options; it is not a middleware pipeline and does not expose handshake machinery.
alternatives_considered: Expose the existing helper-layer packet, TLS, and runtime types directly. Rejected because it would turn implementation seams into the consumer contract.
Introduce a general middleware or event-pipeline surface. Rejected because the repo already uses a fixed-order transport runtime and the public API should stay consumer-facing.
Mirror every helper type from the current source tree. Rejected because the initial cut should stay narrow and baseline-driven.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Options must be treated as configuration bags that are consumed by connect and listen operations, not as live mutable protocol state.

Pending connect, listen, accept, stream-open, and close operations may cancel before commitment; once they commit, the facade must surface the documented terminal outcome instead of retroactively reviving the request.
Public endpoint and address metadata should reuse EndPoint and IPEndPoint; no custom endpoint abstraction is introduced in this slice.
Listener and connect setup must fail fast on missing mandatory endpoint or TLS inputs rather than deferring validation into the transport engine.
The public QuicStreamType stays direction-only, even if the internal stream model tracks initiator separately.
The listener callback is the only server-specific dynamic hook in the public surface.
All TLS transcript, packet-protection, and runtime reducer details remain internal and are mapped to the public facade rather than exposed directly.
data_and_state_considerations: The public options objects are copied into internal connection or listener state when the operation begins. The internal runtime continues to own mutable protocol state, timers, stream accounting, and handshake progress.
risks: If the public API analyzer files are not trimmed in lockstep, helper-layer types could leak into the shipping surface.
The connection and stream facade types must not grow a middleware-style callback surface by accident.
Any future deviation from the Microsoft ref surface should be documented in the design note before implementation.
